subtopics.title: The Lion and the Jewel by Wole Soyinka
common_misconceptions: Lakunle represents progress (he's flawed - superficial modernity)
Baroka is entirely villainous (he's complex, respects tradition)
The play is purely a love story (it's about cultural conflict)
subtopics.teaching_note: Wole Soyinka: Nigerian playwright, 1986 Nobel Prize in Literature (first African). The Lion and the Jewel (1963): set in Ilujinle village. Characters: Baroka (Bale/lion - traditional, cunning), Lakunle (schoolteacher - pseudo-modern, bookish), Sidi (jewel - beautiful, vain), Sadiku (Baroka's senior wife, matchmaker). Themes: Tradition vs Modernity (central conflict), The nature of progress, Deception and manipulation, The power of women (Sadiku's role). Dramatic devices: mime/dance (wedding night scene), flashback (Sidi's photos), comedy, irony. Structure: three scenes - Morning, Noon, Night.

subtopics.title: Selected African Poems
common_misconceptions: Piano and Drums is about music (it's about cultural conflict)
Rubadiri's poem is just about weather (the storm symbolizes political upheaval)
Peters' poem is pessimistic (it's realistic about aging but not hopeless)
subtopics.teaching_note: Gabriel Okara - 'Piano and Drums': speaker torn between traditional African culture (drums, primal) and Western culture (piano, complex, measured). Imagery: 'raw jungle' vs 'wailing piano'. David Rubadiri - 'An African Thunderstorm': describes approaching storm with vivid imagery - wind, trees, clouds, birds. Symbolizes political/social turmoil in Africa. Personification: clouds 'scamper', wind 'whirling'. Lenrie Peters - 'The Panic of Growing Older': reflects on unfulfilled dreams with age. Structure: shows progression from youth's optimism to adult's realization of limitations.

subtopics.title: Selected Non-African Poems
common_misconceptions: Lawrence's 'Snake' is about fear of snakes (it's about human cowardice and respect for nature)
Eliot's poem is just a Nativity story (it's about spiritual journey and death/rebirth)
Blake's poem is only about school (it's about how formal education crushes natural joy)
subtopics.teaching_note: D.H. Lawrence - 'Snake': narrator at water trough meets snake. Inner conflict between human education ('The voice of my education said...') and natural respect. Regrets throwing log at snake. T.S. Eliot - 'Journey of the Magi': from perspective of one Magus. Journey to Bethlehem described as difficult. Theme: death of old self, birth of new. Allusion to Christ's death. William Blake - 'The School Boy': from Songs of Experience. Child who loves summer morning but dreads school. Metaphor: 'cage' for school. Message: formal education stifles natural creativity and joy.
